speaker: interviewer
text: --of the interview with Rachel Huber. It's the 28th of May, 1996. Rachel, you were talking about seeing the peasants digging up the bones.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one of the most shocking things that one can see. What it was, on the way, we passed a place. I do not know how exactly to-- how to pronounce it. I think it was 'kelts' or something like that. And the-- and the-- the cattle wagon was stopped. And-- most probably the fueling or whatever. I do not know why he stopped. And suddenly we heard screams. And there was people screaming, close the wagon. Close the wagon. Close it. They come to kill us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of those that were repatriated were Jewish people that left. And that was a group of Poles who made a pogrom of the remaining Jews in Kielce. And they knew that there were rehabilitation of people coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they went out with hex. Hex? Can you say that English? Hax? Hatchets.

speaker: interviewee
text: What you split wood. How do you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Axes. Hatchets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Axes. Axes you call it, yeah. You know, when I'm excited I can't speak English. I'm sorry. And they wanted to open and kill the people. So we closed the-- very quickly we closed the wagons. And maybe two hours later, the-- the wagon [INAUDIBLE]. But it-- fear, that fear that we are coming into Poland, which is that-- that a pogrom can still happen after the war is to end was a terrible shock for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember of the date of this particular--

speaker: interviewee
text: How could I remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: --experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would have been in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In August 1945. Would have been August, certainly. We finally arrived in a place called Beuthen in Silesia, which is a part of Poland, a province of Poland. In Polish, they call it Bytom.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived there, we were allocated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a flat. And the next thing, we tried-- we looked around, what are we going to do here? And my father immediately started to think how he is going to organize. He's going to open a tailor shop and so on, so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I felt it's no good. I'm just going to finish my life in Beuthen. As young as I was, I felt that a conclusion or a lesson must be learned from what we went through. No more Romania, no more Russia, no more Poland, no more anything. All I wanted is to go to Palestine, to be among my own people. No more chasing, no more running, no more escaping. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I felt that is what I have to look into. And as it so happened, two or three weeks after we were in Beuthen, we heard rumors-- Jews like to talk-- that there are people who organized the so-called Berihah. [HEBREW] in Hebrew means to escape. And there was a organization-- there was a group, not organization I should call-- a group called that were organized from the Jewish Brigade, which was at that time in Europe. What they really wanted was helping all those that come from camps from there and don't have a home and don't have parents or-- or children-- there were thousands and thousands of orphans, young orphans, to give them a home to organize them they should come over to Palestine, not-- to come certainly to Palestine, you can't go because it's was English mandate. But what we wanted to aim to was to organize the so-called Aliyah Bet. That means the illegal Aliyah to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make contact with the Berihah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We made his contact with-- there turned out to be a very young chap, maybe 20 or 21, very young, who said, you know what? I can smuggle you over to Czechoslovakia. And from Czechoslovakia, you in turn will be taken by somebody else that will take you to Austria. And from Austria, we'll take you to Munich. And in Munich, there is the Jewish Brigade there who will look after you. Basically it wasn't the Jewish Brigade there. It was again somebody from-- but it doesn't matter. Again, I was the-- the powerhouse that started to pummel my parents. The little bit that they bought from Tschernowitz, little bit, we had to leave now, because again with the rucksack, we have to go through Europe. And not only that, but my father had severe angina, so that to schlep a person who is sick is not-- But I must confess that I just didn't have the-- the wisdom to think what I do to my father that I can't really pay with his life. But I knew only that I want to live and I don't want to live in Poland and I don't want to live in Europe and I would like to go to Palestine. And after pummeling-- after severe pummeling from their daughter, they-- my parents agreed to go with the rucksack through Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you how we get there. We went on the train, again a organized group. We went on a train. The train stopped middle of the night at some border. When we came to the border, the guide-- I call that young man the guide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not allowed to know even their names. There had to be such a colossal anonymity, we were not allowed to know the name or to call them even. He took us-- he organized the group. He counted as many as he has and said, follow me. And we followed. And we crossed the borders there during the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: On-- how did you cross the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no guards there. There were no guard. The place where he took us was free from guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get across the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked. We walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the station, from the train station, he started walking. We were-- walked several hours that I can remember with my father, which was a colossal enterprise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which border did you cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the border Poland-Czechoslovakia. You must know that was a few months after the war stopped. Things were not organized. The whole of Europe was into a colossal mishmash, a colossal uproar. There-- nothing was stabilized yet. And that could be taken advantage of, really. And we arrived to a train station. He put us on the train. And the train took us [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] again through Bratislava to Vienna. We didn't have any documents. We were not asked for documents, not that I remember that. And we arrived in Vienna. Vienna at that time was half-bombed. Ruins everywhere. The bridges were ruined. It was an awful sight. I can't really confess that I was heartbroken to see that sight. And we were taken to a place that was called the Munchen Museum. It was a bombed-out museum, no windows, no doors, no-- that was there-- I do not know if it was by the UNRRA at that time, which was the United Nations Relief Association, or it was by the Red Cross, because both had so much work to do after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were bunk-- bunks? Yeah? Bunks then and we were allocated a number-- that means that you can get food on that number-- and a bunk and there you are. How long did you stay in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. I remember that I-- we were one night, because I escaped from there with two boys and we went to the Staatsoper to see the Staatsoper. I told you I was young. I was full of energy. I wanted to see. I wanted-- I was not tired. And I think the next day or the day after we were put on a train again. And that would-- would be the hardest thing to cross, and that was Linz, to go on to Linz. In Linz, there was the so-- so to speak, the border of the Russian empire. You see, when they conquered half of Europe, it took them a long while until they completely retreated. Part of Austria remained under the Russian occupation, so to speak, for long, long years until the Russian retreated. And in Linz was the place that the Americans took over. The Russian stopped. So it was the real border between Russia and America, or Russia-- it was American part of Austria. [SIGHS] There it was a little bit more complicated already, because there they had somehow-- we had a new leader, that means I called him leader, that young man, a very nice young man, who told us you go on that train and I have-- he had most probably a paper that this group he can take on the other side. A common--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard to speak. Between 20 and 30, I would say. While we were-- there we are talking about going on a train, we are talking only about cattle wagons, because there were no private, no first class or second class. They were all cattle cars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We mounting those cattle wagons. And when we approached a station that was near the border, that was a station in which the Russian went from-- from wagon to wagon to see-- to count who is going, what is going. Suddenly we heard that alle raus in German. Everybody should come out. As I took always care of my parents, I was the first to jump out in order to take my father down. But as it so happened, I jumped out, and with me several other people, and the train started to move forward. In my screamings for my mother and for my father, I remained there. But all the other people, the train just moved along, which would have been a colossal problem, because I didn't have documents to cross the-- the border. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In we went to the station. That was a little hut there, you know, that the station-- I don't even know the name of that station. And we sat down and we started to-- the other people were in group, maybe 5 or 6 people. And we started to-- to say, what are we going to do? How we are going to do? And for two, three hours, I was pretty low. Only-- my only worry was that my father doesn't have a heart attack there for worry, because I saw that somehow I'm going to make it, somehow. And in the early hours of the morning, very early hours of the morning, I had a-- I heard a call outside. [NON-ENGLISH] That means cattle wagon, Linz. That means that they moved to Linz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I jumped up, took my rucksack, and I called them out, let's go. Let's jump on that. At this station, the-- the cattle wagon was very, very slow at this station. Was probably they-- they waited for-- over some lights. I do not know at the moment why they were so slow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- know only that I jumped on a moving train. It was not moving quickly. It was slowly. But I remember a moment when the knapsack just-- it was a split of a moment I would have been under. But God helped me, and I jumped into that-- that wagon. And a few people jumped after me in that wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew-- did you know that-- where this train was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that it will go to Linz. I didn't know anything else. But I didn't have really a choice. You see, there-- there are situations in life when you don't have a choice. You have to do. And if you don't do, if you are shocked by whatever and you don't do anything, then it works against you. You have to do something and you have to do the first thing that is the opportunity that you get and that you do. And that's what I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that wagon went for hours. And my despair was somehow mounting. I though now my-- my luck is going to really let me down. Who on earth-- where on earth am I going now? And when it stopped, it was stopped at the end of a lot of-- lot of train rails. And in order to come to the station to see where I am, I had to go up and down, up and down on each and every train, because there was no way of asking. There was nobody that I could have asked, where am I? When you jumped onto this train, what did you jump--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they were all standing. They were all standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just jumped in amongst a whole of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: In and out, in and out. Open a door, go down. Open a door, go-- there were maybe four or five of those. And when I came out from the last, my father and my mother were standing on the-- there, waiting, with that particular young man that I call the leader of the group, with that particular young man because he worked it out that if I am not a stupid girl, then I'm going to come somehow to Linz to-- to the station. So he said, you just wait here with me. And we will see. If she will not come, then we will then see what we can organize. But you wouldn't believe it, but two hours later we were taken with the car-- truck to Linz refugee camp that was starting to organize. But there were already groups there. And two hours later, I was dancing a hora with-- with all the others, forgotten what it-- that means to be young. That is the benefit of being young and being enthusiastic and wanting to live. I had such a-- I had all those years of hiding, of war, of that. There was so much [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] suppressed in that, that when it came the end of the-- all I wanted is to go on living and to catch up all those things that I-- that I missed. And in Linz, I remember that we were-- I was at my birthday, I was, means that was the 16th of November.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you? Which birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20. I was 20 then already. Yes, 20 already. And from Linz, we were taken again with the train. There were no more borders, no more question. We're taken to Munchen. And in Munchen, we were in that Deutsche Museum, Munchen Deutsches Museum. Yes. And there again we were registered. And we were asked in which of the-- in which one of the displaced person camps we wanted to go. We didn't have an idea. They were only names and we didn't have an idea. They told us Foehrenwald and Landsberg and-- there were already several there. We didn't know. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you choose?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you how we chose. In the meantime, my father had a chat with-- with other people that were there. And one of those people told him that in Foehrenwald the-- the [GERMAN], the-- the management, in the management is somehow a Jew from Tschernowitz, which is called Herman or whatever. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father decided we will- we'll got to Foehrenwald if there is somebody there from our place of the world. I think that was fate, because we arrived in-- in Foehrenwald in was probably the end of November, '45-- Where was Foehrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Foehrenwald. Foehrenwald was not very far from Munich. It was a place where most probably the Germans had some youth movement or what, because they had the houses there were for group-- rooms and one bigger room, as if they would have built that for-- for youth movement something. We arrived in Foehrenwald. That's quite a drive out of Munich. It is in the so-called Schwarzwald, the beautiful Black Forest. And-- and when wed arrived there, in Foehrenwald, we again were registered, allocated a room, and allocated a number for food. This-- we were fed by the displaced person-- by UNRRA, which were the-- the relief organization. And there we started the real work to be organized, to organize one's self for Aliyah Bet, or for Israel, or contemplating starting to do something with the remnants of European Jewry. There we started to organize, for instance-- as an example one took all the orphans, young children, not so young children, and they were all organized-- we called them kibbutzim, because it was the form that were organized. They were eating together. They were consuming together. They were working. One had, for instance, the washing to do. The other had cleaning and so on. So therefore, they were called kibbutzim. There was-- usually there was a leader of the kibbutz. And the aim was always to go to Israel. The whole organization was the aim was only to go to Israel. At that time, everybody that survived concentration camp or Germans in any form, they all wanted to have-- to go to Israel. Later, when it took years to stay in the displacement camps and there was no way out of going to Palestine, because the English refused to give the certificate, then people started going to America and to Australia and to any other place far away from Europe, only to escape Europe and never to see Europe again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel, what work did you do at Foehrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I tried to help wherever I could, with the little bit-- little bit of schooling that I had, I-- I taught the children. I taught them about geography, about continents, about the sun, about the-- whatever. It was not an organized like a school. But we called it [NON-ENGLISH]. We called it spreche, talks.

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked with the children. And they usually asked me things. I wanted-- what are you interested to know? If I know the answer, then I give the answer. Or about history something, about Jewish history a little bit, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they all were studying Hebrew. We tried the little bit that everybody knew, he-- he shared it with-- with the others. The elderly people-- we had a library, and the elderly people were reading books. The elderly people were organized in political-- the political parties that were Israel were sent so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. And they organized that. Whoever-- my father joined the [NON-ENGLISH] because he was in before the war. And different people did different. There were others who didn't want to do anything, because they were completely apathetic and completely passive. And to-- to go from a-- from years of concentration camp or German oppression and to come to freedom, you have even to adjust yourself to freedom slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how long did you stay in Foehrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Foehrenwald from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, just one minute-- from November '45 'til 1947. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was June 1947 that we went illegal to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say 'we,' who are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I schlepped my father and my mother again, as usual. At that stage, I told him he should stay in Foehrenwald and I will go to Israel and then I can send him a certificate. But what my father said was, what Hitler didn't succeed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't want now to happen. If Hitler has not succeeded in tearing us apart, I don't want-- I will go with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was no way of stopping me, so my father just had to go. I-- I was a horrible person, I suppose. At that time, I already know my husband. Well, my husband-to-be I knew. You met him in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Shmuelik in January 1946, on the 13th of January. That's a date I do remember for a change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was at that time-- he arrived at that kibbutz-- at a kibbutz in Foehrenwald. Just by chance, he took Foehrenwald. And just by chance, he came on that kibbutz where I happened to come. I always used to go for-- on Shabbot, I used to go where the young people are. And there, I was introduced to him. Somebody told me, a very nice [NON-ENGLISH] came. Come and meet him. There I went. There I met him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went on the there were-- you were a group of--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was too. I was with my mother and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you already married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We decided never to marry in Germany. If we marry, we marry only in Israel. And we waited.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was Shmuel--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shmuelik and my father and my mother and myself. We were all part of a wider group. We were not individuals who went. We were part of a-- of a-- of a group. A group that came with that party organized a group. I do not know how many people we were at that time from Foehrenwald. But that was organized. We went by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Munchen through the French border, through [NON-ENGLISH]. We went down, straight down France, near Marseille, a place called Saint-Chamas. There, in a awful place, we were-- we had to be overnight. It was really an awful place. It was hot and it was horrible. And then the next night, we were put on very big trucks and we were taken to the ship. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the ship I don't really know. I know only the name that we gave the ship. We gave the name of the ship [NON-ENGLISH], which was the But basically, that was a ship that was a pleasure steamboat. No. It was not-- maybe it was a steamboat that was later by the-- by the Haganah changed. On the it was called. Then we walked on to the ship. It-- that was all wood. And then we hear the cracking of the wood, because it was old and it was dilapidated. What the Haganah did were they bought that ship. They wanted-- until that particular date, '47, there were-- the Aliyah Bet was only in very small groups, 1,000, 1,500, small ships that were bought somehow and hoped to break through to-- to Palestine. But then the political situation was such that it heated up. Before the Jews wanted that the English would give up the mandate an so on, so they decided it wants a bigger ship. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they decided a ship for 4,500 people. The ship was not big. But what they did, they took out everything inside and they made bunkers. But the bunkers were so-- the bunks were so near one to another that it was really like a sardine you are there. My father slid in and immediately got a heart attack. He couldn't do it. He didn't have any strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so my father was the first in this so-called hospital of the-- of the ship. What was the hospital? That was one very small room that had one bed. And there was somebody who understood I think very little of medicine. But at least my father had breathing space. So what we ended up was that my mother and myself were standing all time next to him, because we looked after him. So we didn't have to be in the bunk. And Shmuelik was-- took on a job of being the one that takes care of the people. There was a rotation of people to come on the deck to breathe, because it was impossible to breathe down there. So Shmuelik was the one that let people through and organized to go, so he had a little bit of breathing ability, a bit of air too.

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip on the was a gruesome trip. The ship was not equipped for the Mediterranean Sea. And not only that, but the ship-- the route that the ship took was straight north-south, north-southeast basically, the southeast to go through the deepest part of the Mediterranean, because the aim was to go to the place that we call Aza and it is now Gaza, the-- of today, to go from there and to come up to Tel Aviv from south to north. They figured out that that would be the best chance that we would have that the ship should come on to Tel Aviv and there, people will run out-- come out onto-- onto the shores and they will mingle with the people that come out and sort of succeed in that part at least should disembark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.
